content.text: Running a language model locally means taking the full inference workload off a remote API and executing it on hardware you control. The piece of software that sits between your application code and the raw model weights — scheduling forward passes, managing the key-value (KV) cache, batching requests, and streaming tokens back — is the inference engine. Two engines dominate the open-source landscape: vLLM, a GPU-optimised high-throughput server, and llama.cpp, a portable C++ runtime that runs everywhere from a data-centre GPU to a developer laptop with no NVIDIA hardware at all. Understanding what each engine does internally, and when to reach for one over the other, is fundamental to building reliable agentic systems that do not depend on third-party API availability, pricing, or data-retention policies.

attrs.alt: Inference Engine Architecture: vLLM vs llama.cpp

content.text: What it is

An inference engine is a runtime that loads a pre-trained neural network into memory, accepts token sequences as input, runs the forward pass of the Transformer stack (or whatever architecture the model uses), and returns a probability distribution over the vocabulary from which the next token is sampled. Repeating this token-by-token loop until a stop condition is met produces text generation. The engine sits immediately above the hardware and immediately below your application or agent loop.

vLLM (Virtual Large Language Model) is an open-source Python/C++ engine developed at UC Berkeley, released in 2023. It introduced PagedAttention — a virtual-memory approach to KV-cache management — that enables continuous batching of many concurrent requests with dramatically higher GPU utilisation than naive implementations. It exposes an OpenAI-compatible REST endpoint out of the box.

llama.cpp is a pure C++ inference library written by Georgi Gerganov, originally created to run Meta's LLaMA models on a MacBook. It defines its own weight format (GGUF) with aggressive integer quantisation (2-bit to 8-bit), and hand-written SIMD kernels for x86, ARM, NVIDIA CUDA, AMD ROCm, Apple Metal, and Vulkan. It ships as a single static binary with no Python dependency and optionally as a local HTTP server.
Why it matters

Every agentic system has a cost, latency, and privacy profile determined largely by how and where it calls an LLM. Cloud APIs are convenient but introduce per-token costs that compound quickly in long ReAct loops, expose every prompt to a third-party provider, and fail unpredictably under rate limits. A locally hosted inference engine removes those constraints:
Costs become fixed infrastructure costs rather than variable per-call costs.
Prompts and completions never leave your network boundary.
You can run models at unlimited concurrency subject only to hardware limits.
You own the upgrade path — swap model weights without changing application code.
Offline and air-gapped deployments become possible (embedded systems, aircraft, submarines, military networks).

For agentic applications specifically, the ability to run hundreds of short internal-monologue calls without paying per token changes what is economically viable. Tasks like self-critique loops, multi-draft reflection, tool-use retries, and scratchpad reasoning all become cheap when the underlying model is local.
Benefits
Data sovereignty: prompts stay on your hardware, meeting GDPR, HIPAA, and SOC 2 requirements without special API agreements.
Zero marginal cost per call: once the server is running, inference is free at the margin — critical for high-frequency agentic inner loops.
Deterministic model version: pin the exact weights and quantisation level; no surprise capability changes from provider-side updates.
Low latency for collocated workloads: when your agent and the model run on the same machine or LAN, round-trip time drops to single-digit milliseconds.
Fine-tuned models: you can load LoRA adapters or fully fine-tuned checkpoints, enabling specialised behaviour (medical, legal, code) without sharing training data.
Edge deployment: llama.cpp runs on Raspberry Pi 4, MacBook Air, iOS, and Android — enabling truly offline applications.
Trade-offs
Capability ceiling: open-weight models still lag frontier closed models (GPT-4o, Claude Opus) on complex reasoning benchmarks; choosing local means accepting a quality ceiling.
Operational burden: you must manage GPU health, driver upgrades, out-of-memory crashes, model updates, and server restarts — none of which are needed with a managed API.
Hardware capex: a server-grade GPU (A100, H100) that matches hosted inference quality costs tens of thousands of dollars; llama.cpp on CPU is accessible but slow for large models.
Quantisation quality loss: llama.cpp's aggressive integer quantisation can degrade instruction-following and reasoning quality, particularly below Q4_K_M for models under 70B.
Cold-start time: loading a 70B model into GPU VRAM can take 30-90 seconds; plan for warm-up time in production deployments.
How it works

Both engines implement the same core autoregressive loop: given a sequence of input token IDs, compute a forward pass through all Transformer layers to produce a logit vector over the vocabulary, apply a sampling strategy (temperature, top-p, top-k) to select the next token, append it to the sequence, and repeat until a stop token is generated or a maximum token budget is exhausted.

The single most expensive computation in each step is the attention mechanism, which requires reading the full KV-cache accumulated so far. The KV-cache stores the key and value projections of every past token so they do not need to be recomputed. Its size grows linearly with sequence length and is bounded by GPU VRAM. Both engines invest heavily in managing this cache efficiently.
vLLM: PagedAttention and Continuous Batching

Traditional LLM inference servers allocate a contiguous block of GPU memory for each request's KV-cache at the start of the request, sized for the maximum possible output length. This wastes memory because most outputs are much shorter than the maximum, and the reserved memory cannot be used by other requests. vLLM's PagedAttention solves this by managing KV-cache memory like a virtual memory system: memory is divided into fixed-size pages (blocks), and a request's KV-cache is stored non-contiguously across available pages. A page table maps logical sequence positions to physical memory blocks, exactly like OS virtual memory paging. This eliminates internal fragmentation and allows near-100% GPU memory utilisation.

Continuous batching (also called iteration-level scheduling) allows vLLM to interleave decode steps from different requests in the same GPU kernel call. When one request finishes generating (hits a stop token), its slot is immediately freed and a new request from the queue is inserted — without waiting for all requests in the batch to finish. This keeps GPU utilisation high even when request lengths vary widely.
llama.cpp: GGUF Quantisation and GGML Compute Graph

llama.cpp stores model weights in the GGUF (GGML Universal Format) binary format, which embeds the model architecture, tokeniser vocabulary, and weight tensors in a single self-contained file. Weights are quantised to integer types (Q4_K_M being the most common balanced choice: 4-bit with K-Means clustering per block, applied to the weight matrix). Each forward pass is expressed as a GGML compute graph — a directed acyclic graph of tensor operations. GGML evaluates this graph lazily, executing hand-tuned SIMD kernels (AVX2 on x86, NEON on ARM) for matrix-vector multiplication, which is the dominant operation during token-by-token decoding. When CUDA or Metal is available, the graph is offloaded to the GPU layer by layer.
Architecture

At the system level, both engines sit between your application and the hardware. The diagram above shows the key internal components. For vLLM: the OpenAI-compatible REST layer receives requests, the scheduler applies continuous batching via the PagedAttention KV-cache manager, and the Tensor Parallel execution engine shards model weight matrices across multiple GPUs via NCCL all-reduce operations. For llama.cpp: the GGUF loader maps weights into memory, the GGML compute graph dispatcher breaks the forward pass into GPU-offloadable chunks, and the llama-server layer (or Ollama on top of it) wraps everything in an HTTP API.

A key architectural difference: vLLM is designed to saturate GPU compute by batching many concurrent requests together. llama.cpp is designed to minimise memory footprint by quantising weights aggressively and evaluating requests one at a time (or in small static batches). This makes them complementary: vLLM for GPU servers with concurrent users; llama.cpp for single-user edge deployments.
When to use it
Choose vLLM when:
You have NVIDIA GPUs with CUDA and want maximum throughput for concurrent requests (APIs, web applications, multi-agent systems calling the model frequently).
You want a drop-in replacement for OpenAI-compatible endpoints — your existing code using the openai Python client requires no changes.
You need speculative decoding to reduce token latency, or tensor parallelism to shard a model too large for a single GPU.
You are running full-precision or GPTQ/AWQ quantised models (not GGUF).
Choose llama.cpp when:
You have no NVIDIA GPU — Apple Silicon, AMD CPU, Raspberry Pi, or a standard cloud VM with only CPU.
You need a self-contained binary with no Python environment to maintain (embedded systems, CI runners, Docker images that must stay small).
You are building developer tooling or CLI applications where a local model runs one task at a time.
You want to use Ollama — which bundles llama.cpp and provides a streamlined model-download and API experience.
How to implement
Running vLLM

# Install: pip install vllm
# Requires: Python 3.9+, CUDA 12.x, NVIDIA GPU with ≥16 GB VRAM for a 7B model

# 1. Start a vLLM server on port 8000 (run from shell)
#    python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
#        --model mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 \
#        --dtype auto \
#        --max-model-len 8192 \
#        --tensor-parallel-size 1

# 2. Call it with the standard openai Python client — zero code changes needed
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(
    base_url="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    api_key="not-needed",  # vLLM accepts any non-empty key
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user",   "content": "Explain PagedAttention in two sentences."},
    ],
    max_tokens=200,
    temperature=0.2,
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

# 3. Streaming response (useful for agentic chat UIs)
stream = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Count to 5 slowly."}],
    max_tokens=60,
    stream=True,
)
for chunk in stream:
    delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content or ""
    print(delta, end="", flush=True)
print()
Running llama.cpp via Ollama
Using llama.cpp Python bindings directly

# pip install llama-cpp-python
# Download a GGUF file: huggingface-cli download bartowski/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-GGUF
#   --include "Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf" --local-dir ./models

from llama_cpp import Llama

# Load model — n_gpu_layers=-1 offloads all layers to GPU if available
llm = Llama(
    model_path="./models/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf",
    n_ctx=4096,          # context window size
    n_gpu_layers=-1,     # -1 = offload all layers; 0 = CPU only
    verbose=False,
)

# Simple completion
output = llm(
    "Q: What is speculative decoding? A:",
    max_tokens=150,
    temperature=0.1,
    stop=["Q:", "\n"],
)
print(output["choices"][0]["text"])

# Chat format (apply_chat_template handled automatically)
response = llm.create_chat_completion(
    messages=[
        {"role": "system",
         "content": "You are a concise technical assistant."},
        {"role": "user",
         "content": "What is Q4_K_M quantisation?"},
    ],
    max_tokens=200,
    temperature=0.2,
)
print(response["choices"][0]["message"]["content"])
Examples

The openai client library is the simplest way to target vLLM because you only change base_url. For an agentic framework like LangChain or LlamaIndex, the same pattern holds — pass base_url to the ChatOpenAI or OpenAI provider and the framework routes all calls locally.

Ollama's OpenAI-compatible prefix (added in v0.1.29) means you can point the openai client at http://localhost:11434/v1 with model set to the Ollama model name. This makes it trivial to swap between a remote Claude endpoint and a local Ollama model in the same code path:
Production / cloud: base_url points to a hosted API endpoint.
Development / offline: base_url points to localhost:11434/v1, model is the local Ollama name.
Testing: a mock server at localhost captures calls without running a real model.
Real-world example

A medical transcription company processes 50,000 clinical consultation recordings per month. Each recording is transcribed by a speech-to-text service, then a local Llama-3-8B model (served via vLLM on two A10G GPUs in an on-premise server) extracts structured clinical entities: chief complaint, medications, diagnoses, and follow-up actions. The extracted JSON feeds directly into the EHR system. Because HIPAA prohibits sending unencrypted patient data to external APIs without a Business Associate Agreement, running vLLM locally is not just a cost optimisation — it is a compliance requirement. The vLLM server handles the peak load of 200 concurrent extraction requests during post-clinic batch processing by using continuous batching, keeping GPU utilisation above 85% throughout.

For individual developers, a common pattern is using Ollama with llama3.2:3b locally during development and switching to a hosted frontier model in production only for the tasks that genuinely need it. This avoids API costs during iteration cycles that may involve hundreds of test calls per hour.
Common mistakes
Choosing too small a model for the task: a Q4_K_M quantised 3B parameter model cannot reliably follow complex multi-step instructions. Match model size to task complexity before optimising for speed.

Ignoring VRAM requirements: a 7B model in float16 requires ~14 GB VRAM; Q4_K_M reduces that to ~4-5 GB, but vLLM also needs VRAM for the KV-cache (which can be as large as the weights themselves under heavy load). Always check both.

Not setting max-model-len on vLLM: the default context length is taken from the model config and may be 128K tokens. The KV-cache for a 128K context at batch size 32 can be many gigabytes. Set --max-model-len to the longest prompt+output you actually need.
Using n_gpu_layers=0 in llama-cpp-python when a GPU is available: this forces CPU-only inference, which is 10-50x slower for larger models. Set n_gpu_layers=-1 to use all available GPU layers.

Running the llama.cpp server on the main application thread: the inference loop blocks for the duration of each request. Always run the server in a separate process or use Ollama's built-in concurrency handling.

Assuming OpenAI-compatible endpoints are fully identical: vLLM and Ollama implement a subset of the OpenAI API. Features like function calling format and logprobs may have slight differences — test the specific fields your agent uses.
Best practices
Pin model versions: specify the exact GGUF filename or HuggingFace commit SHA in your deployment config. Model updates can change generation behaviour.

Use Q4_K_M as the default quantisation for llama.cpp: it offers the best quality/size balance. Only drop to Q3 or Q2 when VRAM or RAM is genuinely constrained, and validate quality on your task before deploying.
Benchmark with realistic workloads: measure time-to-first-token (TTFT) and tokens-per-second (TPS) under your actual concurrency level. Synthetic benchmarks rarely match production traffic patterns.
Add a health-check endpoint to your deployment: vLLM exposes /health; add a readiness probe that checks it before routing traffic to a newly started instance.

Pre-warm the KV-cache for common system prompts: if your agent always starts with a long system prompt, use vLLM's prefix caching feature (--enable-prefix-caching) to avoid recomputing it on every request.

Separate inference from application code: run the engine in a dedicated Docker container or process, accessible over localhost HTTP. This lets you upgrade or restart the model without touching application code.
Performance & scalability

vLLM's continuous batching means throughput scales near-linearly with the number of requests until VRAM is exhausted. A single A100 80 GB running Llama-3-8B can serve over 1,000 tokens per second across a batch of concurrent requests, compared to ~300 tokens per second with a naive single-request-at-a-time server. For models that do not fit on a single GPU, vLLM's tensor parallel mode shards attention heads and feed-forward weight matrices across N GPUs using NCCL, with each GPU handling 1/N of the computation. Pipeline parallelism (layerwise sharding across GPUs) is also supported for very large models.

llama.cpp throughput is limited by CPU memory bandwidth in CPU mode and by GPU compute in GPU offload mode. On an Apple M3 Max (128 GB unified memory), a Q4_K_M 7B model generates approximately 60-80 tokens per second for a single user — sufficient for interactive use. Concurrency is limited because llama.cpp holds one KV-cache in memory per loaded context; supporting multiple concurrent users requires multiple instances of the model, each consuming the same VRAM/RAM footprint.
Key performance levers for vLLM:
--max-num-seqs: maximum concurrent sequences in a batch; increase until VRAM is full.
--gpu-memory-utilization: fraction of GPU VRAM reserved for KV-cache (default 0.9); reducing it frees VRAM for other processes.
--enable-prefix-caching: reuses KV-cache entries for shared prompt prefixes, critical when many requests share the same system prompt.
--speculative-model: a small draft model predicts multiple tokens ahead; the main model verifies them in parallel, reducing latency by up to 2x for greedy decoding.
Tooling & ecosystem
Ollama: packages llama.cpp with a model registry, automatic GGUF downloads, and REST/OpenAI APIs. The easiest path to a local model on a developer machine.
LM Studio: a GUI application that wraps llama.cpp with a model library browser. Good for non-developer users who want a ChatGPT-like interface backed by a local model.
vLLM Docker images: vllm/vllm-openai on Docker Hub provides ready-to-run GPU containers compatible with CUDA 11.8 and 12.x.
SkyPilot: an open-source framework that can deploy vLLM across cloud spot instances, handling failover and cost optimisation automatically.
llama-cpp-python: Python bindings for llama.cpp, installable via pip. Supports CUDA, Metal, OpenBLAS, and CPU backends through compile-time flags.
Hugging Face Hub: the primary source for both GGUF files (look for repositories by bartowski, TheBloke, or official model authors) and HuggingFace-format weights for vLLM.
text-generation-webui: a Gradio-based frontend that supports both llama.cpp and Transformers backends, useful for experimenting with prompts before committing to a production setup.
Comparison with alternatives
The table below compares inference engine choices across the most important operational dimensions:

Security & reliability
Running an inference engine locally shifts the security perimeter from API authentication to network and host security. Key concerns:

Network exposure: vLLM and llama-server bind to 0.0.0.0 by default in some configurations. In production, bind to 127.0.0.1 or use a reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy) with TLS and authentication in front of the inference port.

Prompt injection via tool outputs: a locally hosted model has no built-in guardrails. Sanitise tool outputs before inserting them into the model context, especially if the data comes from the web or user-uploaded files.
Model weight integrity: verify GGUF or HuggingFace model files against their published SHA256 checksums after downloading. A tampered model weight file could embed backdoors.

Out-of-memory crashes: LLM inference uses most of GPU/system VRAM. A long-context request can exceed capacity and crash the server. Set ulimits, use --max-model-len, and monitor GPU memory usage with nvidia-smi or equivalent.

Reliability under load: vLLM will queue requests when the GPU is saturated. Use --max-num-seqs to cap queue depth, and expose a /health endpoint for load-balancer health checks to drain connections before a restart.
GGUF version compatibility: llama.cpp updates may deprecate older GGUF quantisation types. Pin the llama.cpp version that matches the GGUF files you use, or re-quantise models when upgrading.
Key takeaways
An inference engine is the runtime layer between model weights and your application; choosing the right one determines latency, throughput, cost, and compatibility.
vLLM's PagedAttention and continuous batching make it the best choice for GPU servers with concurrent traffic; its OpenAI-compatible API requires no code changes.
llama.cpp's GGUF quantisation makes 7B-70B models accessible on consumer hardware; Ollama is its friendliest distribution for developers.
Local inference is a compliance requirement for data that cannot leave your network, not just a cost optimisation.
The two engines are complementary: use llama.cpp locally during development and vLLM (or a hosted API) in production, sharing the same OpenAI-compatible interface.
Always benchmark under realistic concurrency and measure both time-to-first-token (TTFT) and sustained tokens-per-second before committing to an engine choice.
Mental model

Think of an inference engine as a database server — but instead of executing SQL queries against rows, it executes forward passes against token sequences. Just as you would never embed a raw SQLite file in your web application and call read() on it yourself, you do not directly manipulate model weight tensors in your agent code. The inference engine abstracts away hardware, batching, memory management, and protocol details, exposing a clean HTTP interface that makes the model look like a stateless function: tokens in, tokens out. vLLM is the PostgreSQL of this world — powerful, high-throughput, requiring a proper server setup. llama.cpp is SQLite — lightweight, embeddable, and enough for a single user or small workload.
